item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably. As such, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In fairness, this would hardly be the biggest coverup the foundation's ever done - a few more amnestetics, some doctoring of files and it should be fine

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty much why I like this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upon a re-read, I like this more than I did two years ago. Yes, there isn't a whole lot here, and it's inherently limited by the box that Aelanna forced herself to write in, but this is a case where the concept is enough to sell me with its implications. Changed from neutral vote to +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In addition to the fridge-horror that Litfried noted, there is the somewhat prescient ability to know that the driver will die from the accident, regardless of his/her immediate condition. I find that a little spooky as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. I think it's a perfect example of fridge horror, and I'm surprised no-one has pointed this out yet.

Imagine you're an ordinary person, just living your life. You're driving home from work, a bit distracted because it was a tough day, and you're looking forward to getting home to your spouse and kids, when BAM!

It all happens in slow motion. The truck comes swerving across the motorway, and you know - just know - that you aren't going to be able to avoid it. It's going to hit you. And you're going to die.

And then you wake up. You can't believe it. You survived after all! You're even remarkably unhurt! You can see your spouse and kids again, and they're going to be sooo happy!

But wait. This - this isn't a hospital…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They are contained. They just aren't killed. Basically they live out their lives fairly comfortably in a small--but still comfortable--cell, and are presumably brought a few novels every other month.

As far as the Ethics Committee, a lot of canon has the EC being the most powerful force in the Foundation. Not because the Foundation is super-nice, but because it is not pointlessly cruel, and the Ethics Committee is there to ensure that it does not become pointlessly cruel. Their job is to look at things and decide if the cost is worth it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna go against the grain and say that I like the note. There's so many articles that paint the Foundation as dark and edgy for dark and edgy's sake, so this is a nice departure from that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I really can't see what the point of this is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well put.
They may well be truly harmless, but they are still a thing that should not be. The Foundation would contain, or otherwise deal with them, no matter what the Ethics Committee says.

Of course, the idea that the EC would have any real authority within such an organization does not ring true to me in and of itself, so I might be biased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept but I feel like the note at the end is a bit misplaced. Remember, even if they are completely normal, these clones of humans are SCPs, and are therefore not considered human by the foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first thought: what would happen if SCP-1676-1 had a head-on collision with an identical non-anomalous car, both with drivers that weren't buckled in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I highly recommend dumping or else completely revising the final addendum. That said, I wonder what happens if there's a -1 in the driver's seat and another materializes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'd think the Foundation would just go and buy out or straight up trash all non anomalous versions of the car

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will echo the above sentiments: I don't hate it, but it just didn't "do it" for me. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has an effect which feels just random, but doesn't really build to anything interesting. The idea that they'd consider using the randomly appearing humans as d-class is just silly, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's well written, but it feels a bit flat. I would personally like to know more about the duplicates. What do they think about what happened? To them is it just like if they re-appeared in a different location at the time of accident?

Could the Foundation simply arrange for a recall of all or most of the remaining cars? Would that be cheaper in the long run instead of containing the duplicate people?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
an exact duplicate of the driver will appear in the driver's seat of SCP-1676-1 precisely 37 hours and 21 minutes later

Is SCP-1676-2 a duplicate of the recently-living driver, or the dead driver from the collision? At first I assumed it would be just a splattered corpse appearing in the car, but the rest of the article suggests that SCP-1676-2s are alive, so you may want to clarify that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. I think this needs a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like this one and can't really find any aspects if it that are interesting to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid this didn't do too much for me. I like the core concept, but it almost feels like the author herself wasn't terribly sure where she wanted to go with it, and so raised a few potential plot threads, but followed none of them.

No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except that the original driver is still in existence, and still dead. You have the family to worry about, police officers, EMTs/firefighters, and all of the attendant witnesses that go with car accidents. A few incidents might be easily wrapped up, but I really doubt the Foundation can cover up all of them.

Besides, while we can't find any difference between -2 instances and the original subject, we will never be completely sure that there's nothing wrong with them. no matter how much a -2 looks, talks, and walks like the original, the original is still a mangled corpse in a morgue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a mechanic by trade so I have a special place in my little black heart for vehicle skips. I particularly like this one. The only thing I would suggest, and this is only a suggestion, perhaps have them check the odometer for signs of tampering to verify it has never been driven. But this is just nitpicking and I enjoyed it so up vote for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one coz it's odd. Not scary, not dangerous. Just odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. The duplicates should be given amnesiacs and released. The rest is well written and enjoyable, so upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll second the sentiments that the addendum is out of place. I can't imagine why the Foundation would even consider killing the specimens as opposed to, say, giving them amnesiac therapy and releasing them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree the note doesn't really add anything to the article, but it doesn't really take anything away from the article either. Since I already really liked the effect by the time I got there, I'm going to give this an upvote. It makes sense that the if someone is going to perform resurrections, they'll probably be doing it to make money, somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find the backstory implied interesting, as I'm wondering what exactly SCP-1676 are. This can most definitely be expanded upon, but as it is I like the concept and the writing. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The note at the end didn't added much for me, but I like the concept and the simplicity of this enough to up-vote it. Particularly how "Customer Loyalty Program" implies a lot of back story through the object's description.

E: spelling + grammar

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't feel the note at the end adds anything. Other than that, it's mildly interesting, but doesn't leave enough of an impact for me to upvote. I would like to see it fleshed out more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hurm. I thought on this one as I read it, but ultimately I don't think it adds up to enough or ties the concept together well enough to be interesting. I sort of like the premise of the drivers being replaced by copies, and that test at the end was interesting… but it ultimately failed to add up to anything significant. The log at the end about the ethics committee almost feels like its coming from another SCP, with the way it ties into what came before it. There's just no single unifying element or theme to make me care about this.
